description: products-ms observability: cache efficiency, DB latency, Redis health, process memory.
title: products-ms Observability

panels.title: Cache Hit Ratio
panels.description:
What fraction of reads were served from Redis without touching the database.

1.0 (100%) = perfect — every read was a cache hit.
Drops after a deploy (cold cache) or after heavy writes (many invalidations) are normal.
A sustained low ratio means TTLs are too short or write volume is very high.

Note: 'products' label = findAll list keys; 'product' label = findOne item keys.

targets.expr:
sum(rate(products_ms_cache_hits_total[5m])) by (entity, operation)
/
(
  sum(rate(products_ms_cache_hits_total[5m])) by (entity, operation)
  +
  sum(rate(products_ms_cache_misses_total[5m])) by (entity, operation)
)

panels.title: DB Query Duration — p50 / p95 / p99
panels.description:
How long the database takes to answer when the cache misses (or when withDeleted=true bypasses the cache).

p50 (median): half of all queries are faster than this.
p95: 95% of queries are faster — a good SLO target.
p99: worst-case tail — spikes here indicate occasional slow queries (index scans, lock contention, cold connection pool).

A flat, low p99 with a higher p99 spike = temporary issue. A rising p99 baseline = investigate query plans.

panels.title: Redis Memory — used vs maxmemory cap
panels.description:
How much RAM Redis is currently using vs the hard cap set via --maxmemory 256mb in docker-compose.

When used_memory reaches maxmemory, Redis starts evicting keys according to the volatile-lru policy (evicts least-recently-used keys that have a TTL set — which is all of our cache keys).

Healthy headroom: keep used_memory below ~80% of maxmemory. If it consistently hits the cap, either raise maxmemory or reduce cache TTLs.

panels.title: Redis Evicted Keys Rate
panels.description:
Keys evicted per second. Eviction fires when Redis reaches maxmemory and a new write arrives.

With volatile-lru policy: Redis removes the least-recently-used key that has a TTL — so cache keys are the first to go, which is correct behaviour.

A non-zero rate here means the cache is under memory pressure. Acceptable in short bursts; sustained eviction = cache is effectively smaller than you think, hurting your hit ratio.

Fix: raise maxmemory, reduce TTLs, or add Redis memory to the server.

panels.title: products-ms Process Memory (RSS)
panels.description:
Resident Set Size — the physical RAM the products-ms Node.js process holds in the container.

Expect a gradual rise at startup as NestJS and TypeORM initialise, then a stable plateau.

A plateau that keeps climbing over hours without traffic growth = memory leak. Common culprits in NestJS:
  - Event listeners added but never removed
  - TypeORM query results cached in application memory
  - Large arrays kept in module-level variables

Filtered to job='products-ms' so this panel shows only this service even if other microservices are instrumented later.
